## [F] Kabbalistic Tree of Life / Hermetic-Alchemical Models


## 1. Basic Identification
**Name:** Kabbalistic Tree of Life / Hermetic-Alchemical Models
**Author / Origin:** Jewish Mysticism (Kabbalah, e.g., Zohar, Isaac Luria) and Western Esotericism / Hermeticism (e.g., Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, Carl Jung's psychological alchemy).
**Period:** Roots in 1st centuries CE (Merkabah mysticism), crystallized in 12th-13th century (Zohar), revolutionized in 16th century (Lurianic Kabbalah), and synthesized with Hermeticism/Alchemy in the 19th-20th centuries.
**Field:** Mysticism, Esotericism, Depth Psychology, Ontology, Theology.
**Primary Scale:** Spiritual / Individual / Cosmic (Microcosm-Macrocosm).

## 2. Purpose
This model solves the problem of how the Infinite, unknowable Divine (Ein Sof) relates to and manifests the finite, physical world, and conversely, how the human soul can ascend back to divine unity. It provides a map of consciousness, creation, and transformation for mystics, esotericists, and depth psychologists. It offers a structured pathway for spiritual evolution, psychological integration (individuation), and understanding the underlying architecture of reality.

## 3. Explicit Structure
The model employs several interconnected structural frameworks to map reality and transformation. The foundational structure is the 10 Sefirot, which are divine emanations. These include Kether (Crown, pure being), Chokmah (Wisdom, active force), Binah (Understanding, receptive matrix), Chesed (Mercy, expansive love), Geburah (Severity, limitation and judgment), Tiferet (Beauty, harmony and balance), Netzach (Victory, emotion and instinct), Hod (Splendor, intellect and structure), Yesod (Foundation, subconscious and connection), and Malkuth (Kingdom, physical reality). Da'at (Knowledge) exists as a hidden or quasi-sefirah representing the abyss.

These Sefirot are organized into Three Pillars. The Pillar of Mercy on the right represents expansion and active, masculine forces. The Pillar of Severity on the left represents constriction and passive, feminine forces. The Pillar of Equilibrium in the center represents balance and integration.

Reality is further divided into Four Worlds (Olamot). Atziluth is the world of Emanation, associated with spirit and fire. Briah is the world of Creation, associated with intellect and air. Yetzirah is the world of Formation, associated with emotion and water. Assiah is the world of Action, associated with the physical body and earth.

In the Hermetic-Alchemical tradition, the process of transformation (the Magnum Opus) is mapped through four stages. Nigredo (Blackening) involves decomposition, catharsis, and ego death. Albedo (Whitening) brings purification, illumination, and clarity. Citrinitas (Yellowing) represents awakening and the integration of new archetypes. Finally, Rubedo (Reddening) signifies completion, union, rebirth, and the attainment of the Philosopher's Stone.

## 4. Implicit Ontology
Reality is divided into a descending hierarchy of emanations from pure, infinite spirit down to dense, finite matter, and an ascending path of return. It is also divided laterally into dualistic forces, such as expansion versus constriction, which are mediated by a balancing center.

The fundamental building blocks of this ontology are divine light or energy, archetypal principles represented by the Sefirot, the classical elements of Fire, Air, Water, and Earth, and the dynamic tension between opposites.

The model considers the spiritual, the psychological, the archetypal, the energetic, and the process of inner transformation to be essential domains.

Conversely, the purely material, mundane, or socio-political world is seen as secondary. It is viewed as the lowest emanation (Malkuth or Assiah), serving as a reflection or receptacle of higher spiritual realities rather than the primary source of truth.

The model completely leaves out modern empirical science, technological infrastructure, complex socio-economic systems, ecological biodiversity (except as symbolic elements), and secular governance.

## 5. Layer Logic
The deep logic of the model is based on emanation and return, representing involution and evolution, as well as dialectic synthesis. It moves from the subtle and infinite to the dense and finite during creation, and from the fragmented and unconscious to the unified and conscious during spiritual ascent or alchemy. It operates on the Hermetic principle of "As above, so below," where the microcosm reflects the macrocosm, meaning that psychological and spiritual transformation mirrors the cosmic structure of the Tree of Life.

## 6. Covered Domains
| Domain | Rating | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| body/physical survival | 1 | Malkuth/Assiah |
| energy | 2 | Divine light, energetic pathways |
| water | 1 | Symbolic element of Yetzirah/Albedo |
| food | 0 | |
| health | 1 | Spiritual/psychological wholeness |
| habitat | 0 | |
| infrastructure | 0 | |
| mobility | 0 | |
| materials | 1 | Alchemical base matter |
| ecology | 0 | |
| biodiversity | 0 | |
| economy | 0 | |
| work | 0 | Except as "The Great Work" / Magnum Opus |
| value creation | 0 | |
| entrepreneurship | 0 | |
| finance | 0 | |
| governance | 1 | Cosmic/divine governance, laws of creation |
| law/justice | 1 | Geburah represents divine judgment/law |
| security | 0 | |
| community | 1 | Secondary to individual spiritual ascent |
| relationships | 1 | Dynamic between masculine/feminine principles |
| family | 0 | |
| education | 1 | Esoteric transmission of knowledge |
| culture | 1 | |
| art | 1 | Tarot, symbolism |
| media | 0 | |
| communication | 1 | The Word, Hebrew letters as creative forces |
| knowledge | 2 | Da'at, esoteric wisdom |
| science | 0 | Modern science absent; occult science present |
| technology | 0 | |
| AI/computation | 0 | |
| ethics | 2 | The Ethical Triad: Chesed, Geburah, Tiferet |
| meaning | 2 | Central to the entire system |
| spirituality | 2 | The core of the model |
| consciousness | 2 | Map of consciousness |
| future/foresight | 1 | Prophetic elements, Tikkun Olam/repair of the world |
| planetary direction | 1 | Tikkun Olam |
| civilizational purpose | 1 | Restoration of divine harmony |

## 7. Missing Domains
Absent domains include modern science, technology, economics, ecology, practical governance, and physical infrastructure. This is simply out of scope; the model is an esoteric, mystical, and psychological map of consciousness and divine emanation, not a sociological or physical blueprint for civilizational management.

## 8. Biases
The model exhibits a strong Spiritual and Mystical bias, prioritizing the unseen, divine, and esoteric over the tangible and empirical. Especially in its Hermetic and Jungian interpretations, it heavily biases psychological inner transformation and individuation. It is Anthropocentric and Microcosmic, viewing the human being as the center and mirror of the cosmos (Adam Kadmon). The model is inherently Emanationist and Hierarchical, assuming a top-down flow of reality from a perfect source to an imperfect material world. Finally, it is Western-centric, being deeply rooted in Abrahamic and Mediterranean or European esoteric traditions.

## 9. Contribution to Elysium
The major idea this model brings is the architecture of consciousness, the necessity of balancing opposing forces (expansion and constraint) through a synthesizing center, and the structured stages of profound transformation from Nigredo to Rubedo.

The concept of "Tikkun" (repair and integration) and the multi-layered mapping of reality across Action, Emotion, Intellect, and Spirit could enrich the Elysium ontology by providing a framework for how different civilizational layers interact and require alignment.

It provides a warning against getting lost in the "Abyss" (Da'at) of pure intellect without grounded experience, and highlights the danger of imbalance, whether that is too much expansion (Chesed) or too much restriction (Geburah). Its primary limitation is its abstraction and detachment from practical, material, and ecological realities.

While primarily useful for the Human scale in terms of psychological and spiritual development, its principles of balance and multi-layered reality can be metaphorically applied to the Society and Civilization scales.

## 10. Mapping to Provisional 7 Layers
| Layer | Rating | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Matter / Foundation | 1 | Represented by Malkuth and Assiah, but viewed as the lowest, densest emanation rather than the primary focus. |
| Life / Vitality | 1 | Represented by Yesod and Netzach, the energetic and instinctual drives, but framed in esoteric terms. |
| Agency / Power | 2 | The dynamic tension between Chesed (Expansion) and Geburah (Restriction) defines agency and willpower. |
| Connection / Cohesion | 2 | Tiferet acts as the central hub of cohesion; the 22 paths represent connections between states of being. |
| Expression / Governance | 1 | Hod represents structure and language; divine governance is mapped, but not human political governance. |
| Knowledge / Vision | 2 | Chokmah (Wisdom), Binah (Understanding), and Da'at (Knowledge) are the highest accessible cognitive and spiritual states. |
| Consciousness / Meaning | 2 | The entire Tree of Life and the Alchemical Magnum Opus are ultimate maps of consciousness, meaning, and spiritual realization. |

## 11. Key Sources
The foundational text of Kabbalah is *The Zohar*. Other key sources include *Etz Chayim* (Tree of Life) by Isaac Luria and Chaim Vital, *The Mystical Qabalah* by Dion Fortune, *Psychology and Alchemy* by C.G. Jung, *A Garden of Pomegranates* by Israel Regardie, *Kabbalah: New Perspectives* by Moshe Idel, and *Major Trends in Jewish Mysticism* by Gershom Scholem.


---

## [G] Gross National Happiness — Bhutan


## 1. Basic Identification
**Name:** Gross National Happiness — Bhutan
**Author / Origin:** Jigme Singye Wangchuck (4th King of Bhutan) / Centre for Bhutan Studies
**Period:** 1972 (conceptualized), 2008 (institutionalized in Constitution), 2011 (Alkire-Foster method index)
**Field:** Development Economics, Public Policy, Wellbeing Metrics, Societal Governance
**Primary Scale:** Society / Civilization

## 2. Purpose
The Gross National Happiness (GNH) model addresses the fundamental limitations of reductionist, purely economic measures of national progress, such as Gross Domestic Product (GDP), which systematically ignore human wellbeing, ecological health, and cultural vitality. It was originally developed for the nation of Bhutan to guide its modernization and development policies in a manner that preserves its 1,200-year Buddhist heritage, natural environment, and social cohesion. 

In a broader global context, GNH serves as a pioneering alternative paradigm for sustainable development. It demonstrates that a government can systematically measure and optimize for holistic human flourishing rather than mere material accumulation, providing a comprehensive framework for nations seeking to balance economic growth with social and environmental imperatives.

## 3. Explicit Structure
The GNH model is explicitly structured around a hierarchical framework consisting of 4 Pillars, which are further elaborated into 9 Domains. These domains are then quantified using 33 specific Indicators.

The four foundational pillars are: Sustainable and equitable socio-economic development, Environmental conservation, Preservation and promotion of culture, and Good governance. These pillars form the philosophical basis of the model.

The nine domains and their corresponding focus areas are detailed below:

| Domain | Key Indicators & Focus Areas |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Psychological Well-being** | Life satisfaction, positive and negative emotions, spirituality |
| **Health** | Self-reported health, healthy days, disability, mental health |
| **Time Use** | Balance between work and sleep |
| **Education** | Literacy, schooling, knowledge, values |
| **Cultural Diversity & Resilience** | Artistic skills (Zorig Chusum), cultural participation, native language, harmony (Driglam namzha) |
| **Good Governance** | Political participation, services, governance performance, fundamental rights |
| **Community Vitality** | Donation of time and money, safety, community relationships, family cohesion |
| **Ecological Diversity & Resilience** | Wildlife damage, urban issues, responsibility toward environment, ecological issues |
| **Living Standards** | Per capita income, assets, housing quality |

## 4. Implicit Ontology
The GNH model divides reality into a holistic, interdependent web where material conditions, such as living standards and physical health, are inextricably linked with interior states like psychological wellbeing and spirituality. It equally integrates the social fabric, encompassing community, culture, and governance, with the natural world and ecological resilience.

Its fundamental building blocks are the "conditions for happiness" or "causes of wellbeing," viewed through a Buddhist lens of balance, often referred to as the Middle Way. The ontology treats time, nature, culture, and the human mind as entities that are equally real, vital, and measurable as financial capital and physical infrastructure.

The model considers the integration of material and non-material development as absolutely essential. Psychological wellbeing, ecological resilience, and cultural continuity are treated as foundational prerequisites for a healthy society, not as secondary luxuries to be pursued only after economic wealth is achieved. Conversely, pure economic growth and individualistic material accumulation are considered secondary. Income is viewed merely as a threshold condition for sufficiency rather than an open-ended, infinite goal.

Consequently, the model largely leaves out aggressive technological acceleration, global geopolitical dominance, hyper-individualism, and purely secular or materialist conceptions of progress. It does not emphasize innovation for its own sake, nor does it address systemic civilizational risks beyond the scope of ecological conservation and cultural erosion.

## 5. Layer Logic
The deep logic of the GNH framework is rooted in **balance and interdependence**, reflecting the Buddhist concept of the Middle Way. Rather than proposing a linear progression—such as Maslow's hierarchy of needs, where material requirements must be satisfied before spiritual ones can be addressed—GNH posits a simultaneous, interdependent matrix of human needs.

It operates on an **interior↔exterior** and **individual↔collective↔ecological** logic. In this paradigm, happiness is not viewed as an isolated, individual psychological state. Instead, it is understood as an emergent property of a balanced system where the individual's mind, the community's cultural practices, the government's policies, and the earth's ecology exist in dynamic harmony.

## 6. Covered Domains
The following table rates the presence of various domains within the GNH model (2 = central, 1 = present but secondary, 0 = absent):

| Domain | Rating | Domain | Rating | Domain | Rating |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Body/Physical Survival | 2 | Value Creation | 1 | Art | 2 |
| Energy | 1 | Entrepreneurship | 0 | Media | 0 |
| Water | 1 | Finance | 1 | Communication | 1 |
| Food | 1 | Governance | 2 | Knowledge | 2 |
| Health | 2 | Law/Justice | 2 | Science | 1 |
| Habitat | 2 | Security | 2 | Technology | 0 |
| Infrastructure | 1 | Community | 2 | AI/Computation | 0 |
| Mobility | 1 | Relationships | 2 | Ethics | 2 |
| Materials | 1 | Family | 2 | Meaning | 2 |
| Ecology | 2 | Education | 2 | Spirituality | 2 |
| Biodiversity | 2 | Culture | 2 | Consciousness | 2 |
| Economy | 1 | Future/Foresight | 1 | Planetary Direction | 1 |
| Work | 2 | Civilizational Purpose | 2 | | |

## 7. Missing Domains
Important domains that are notably absent from the GNH framework include advanced technology, artificial intelligence and computation, media and information ecology, entrepreneurship, and global or planetary governance.

This absence is largely a reflection of the model's original scope, as it was designed as a national policy framework for a small, agrarian, and deeply traditional Himalayan kingdom. However, when evaluated as a universal civilizational model, the lack of a framework for navigating exponential technology, digital information spheres, and complex globalized financial systems represents a significant limitation. GNH is fundamentally a model of preservation, sufficiency, and balance, rather than a model equipped for technological navigation or systemic disruption.

## 8. Biases
The GNH model exhibits several distinct biases inherent to its origins and philosophy. It is deeply **Spiritual and Religious**, rooted firmly in Mahayana Buddhist principles such as karma, compassion, non-violence, and the Middle Way. It possesses a strong **Ecological** bias, placing immense emphasis on harmony with nature and environmental conservation.

Furthermore, the model is explicitly **Anti-Materialist and Anti-Developmentalist**, rejecting the Western, GDP-driven, hyper-capitalist paradigm of endless economic growth. It is also highly **Traditionalist and Cultural**, biased toward the preservation of specific local traditions, such as the Driglam namzha (code of etiquette), native languages, and traditional arts. Critics have occasionally noted that this strong cultural focus can border on homogenization or the exclusion of minority groups. Finally, it has a **Holistic and Psychological** bias, operating on the assumption that subjective wellbeing and time balance are valid, quantifiable metrics that should direct state policy.

## 9. Contribution to Elysium
The major contribution of the GNH model to the Elysium ontology is the operationalization and quantification of holistic wellbeing. It provides empirical proof that subjective states, such as psychological wellbeing, time use, and cultural vitality, can be rigorously measured and utilized to drive state policy and resource allocation at a national level.

Specifically, the category of "Time Use" could significantly enrich the Elysium ontology as a fundamental metric of civilizational health. Additionally, the explicit inclusion of "Cultural Resilience" and "Psychological Wellbeing" as equal pillars alongside material living standards offers a more robust framework for human flourishing.

The model provides a critical warning: a civilization driven purely by GDP and economic metrics will optimize for cancer-like growth at the direct expense of its host—namely, the ecology, the community, and the human mind. However, its limitation lies in its heavy focus on cultural preservation and harmony, suggesting that such a model might struggle to integrate or survive rapid technological disruption or intense external geopolitical pressures. The GNH framework is highly useful for both the Society and Civilization scales, with direct mapping to the Human scale, as its metrics are fundamentally gathered at the individual level.

## 10. Mapping to Provisional 7 Layers
The following table maps the GNH model to the provisional 7 layers, rated on a scale of 0 to 2 (2 = strongly supported, 1 = indirectly supported, 0 = absent):

| Layer | Rating | Justification |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Matter / Foundation** | 2 | Living standards, housing, income, and assets are explicitly measured as foundational threshold requirements for wellbeing. |
| **Life / Vitality** | 2 | Health, healthy days, ecological diversity, and environmental conservation are central pillars of the framework. |
| **Agency / Power** | 1 | Good governance and fundamental rights provide agency, but the model emphasizes collective harmony and duty over individualistic power or disruption. |
| **Connection / Cohesion** | 2 | Community vitality, family, relationships, and cultural participation are explicitly measured and highly valued domains. |
| **Expression / Governance** | 2 | Good governance is one of the four main pillars; cultural expression via traditional arts is a key indicator of societal health. |
| **Knowledge / Vision** | 2 | Education, literacy, schooling, and knowledge constitute a dedicated domain within the index. |
| **Consciousness / Meaning** | 2 | Psychological wellbeing, spirituality, positive/negative emotions, and values are central, distinguishing this model from almost all conventional development frameworks. |

## 11. Key Sources
- Ura, K., Alkire, S., Zangmo, T., & Wangdi, K. (2012). *A Short Guide to Gross National Happiness Index*. Centre for Bhutan Studies.
- Alkire, S., & Zangmo, T. (2023). *Beyond GDP: Bhutan’s GNH Index Unveiling the Path to Human Flourishing*. Multidimensional Poverty Peer Network (MPPN) / Oxford Poverty and Human Development Initiative (OPHI).
- Centre for Bhutan and GNH Studies (Official Website and Reports).
- Constitution of the Kingdom of Bhutan (2008), Article 9.
- United Nations General Assembly Resolution 65/309 (2011): *Happiness: towards a holistic approach to development*.

## [G] Buen Vivir / Sumak Kawsay — Andean Indigenous Philosophy


## 1. Basic Identification
**Name:** Buen Vivir / Sumak Kawsay — Andean Indigenous Philosophy

**Author / Origin:** Indigenous peoples of the Andes and Amazon (particularly Kichwa and Aymara), later articulated by Latin American intellectuals (e.g., Alberto Acosta, Eduardo Gudynas) and incorporated into the constitutions of Ecuador (2008) and Bolivia (2009).

**Period:** Ancestral origins, with modern political and academic articulation emerging in the late 1990s and 2000s.

**Field:** Indigenous Philosophy, Political Ecology, Post-Development Theory, Decoloniality.

**Primary Scale:** Society / Planet (with strong roots in Community and Individual harmony).

## 2. Purpose
Buen Vivir solves the problem of the destructive, anthropocentric, and individualistic nature of Western capitalist development. It provides an alternative paradigm for indigenous communities, social movements, and nations seeking to escape the ecological and social crises caused by extractivism and neoliberalism. In its context, it serves as a decolonial tool to assert indigenous worldviews, promote the rights of nature, and redefine well-being not as material accumulation, but as harmonious coexistence within a living cosmos.

## 3. Explicit Structure
The model is explicitly structured around three core dimensions of harmony, often articulated in the Kichwa Amazonian tradition. The first is **Sacha Runa Yachay**, which represents harmony with oneself, encompassing inner strength, wisdom, and balanced conduct. The second is **Runakuna Kawsay**, which signifies harmony with the community, built upon solidarity, reciprocity, and equity. The third is **Sumak Allpa**, which denotes harmony with nature, emphasizing respect for the living environment and sustainable interaction with the territory.

Additionally, Buen Vivir operates on three political and discursive levels. The Indigenist or Pachamamist level focuses on ancestral identity, spirituality, and cosmovision. The Socialist or Communitarian level focuses on equity, redistribution, and collective welfare over individual profit. Finally, the Ecologist or Post-developmentalist level focuses on sustainability, biocentrism, and the Rights of Nature.

## 4. Implicit Ontology
In the ontology of Buen Vivir, reality is not divided into a nature-society dichotomy. Instead, it is perceived as a single, interconnected, living organism (Pacha or Pachamama) where humans are merely one component of a broader community of life. The fundamental "building blocks" of this reality are relationships and interconnectedness (relationality), rather than isolated objects or individuals. Everything has life and agency, including mountains, rivers, and spirits.

The model considers community (Ayllu), territory, reciprocity, solidarity, ecological balance, and spirituality as essential domains. Conversely, material accumulation, economic growth, individual property, and technological progress are considered secondary and are subordinated to collective and ecological well-being. The concept completely leaves out the idea of linear, infinite "development" or "progress," the commodification of nature (natural capital), anthropocentrism, and methodological individualism.

## 5. Layer Logic
The deep logic of Buen Vivir is relational and biocentric, moving from the individual to the collective, and ultimately to the planetary or cosmic level. A person cannot achieve Buen Vivir without harmony in the household, which is necessary for harmony in the community, which in turn must exist in harmony with the natural world (Pachamama). It is a logic of interdependence and reciprocity, where the material and spiritual are inseparable.

## 6. Covered Domains
| Domain | Rating | Domain | Rating | Domain | Rating |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| body/physical survival | 2 | economy | 1 | relationships | 2 |
| energy | 1 | work | 1 | family | 2 |
| water | 2 | value creation | 1 | education | 2 |
| food | 2 | entrepreneurship | 0 | culture | 2 |
| health | 2 | finance | 0 | art | 1 |
| habitat | 2 | governance | 2 | media | 0 |
| infrastructure | 1 | law/justice | 2 | communication | 1 |
| mobility | 0 | security | 1 | knowledge | 2 |
| materials | 1 | community | 2 | science | 1 |
| ecology | 2 | technology | 0 | ethics | 2 |
| biodiversity | 2 | AI/computation | 0 | meaning | 2 |
| spirituality | 2 | consciousness | 2 | future/foresight | 1 |
| planetary direction | 2 | civilizational purpose | 2 | | |

*(Ratings: 2=central, 1=present but secondary, 0=absent)*

## 7. Missing Domains
The model largely omits modern technological domains, including AI, advanced infrastructure, global finance, mass mobility, and media. This is not a flaw but a deliberate limitation; Buen Vivir is a localized, place-based philosophy that resists the universalizing, technocratic, and hyper-connected nature of globalized modernity. It focuses on the local territory and community scale, leaving global technological and financial systems out of its scope.

## 8. Biases
Buen Vivir exhibits several distinct biases. It is strongly **Biocentric and Ecocentric**, prioritizing the living earth over human supremacy. It is **Communitarian**, favoring the collective over the individual. It is inherently **Anti-capitalist and Anti-developmentalist**, strongly opposing Western notions of linear progress and material accumulation. Furthermore, it is **Spiritual and Animist**, viewing the natural world as imbued with spirit and agency. Finally, it is **Decolonial and Indigenous-centric**, centering Andean and Amazonian indigenous epistemologies against Eurocentric modernity.

## 9. Contribution to Elysium
The major idea Buen Vivir brings to the Elysium framework is the dissolution of the nature-society divide, the recognition of Nature as a subject of rights (Rights of Nature), and the definition of well-being as relational harmony rather than material accumulation. It could enrich the Elysium ontology by introducing "Relationality" or "Biocentrism" as a foundational category, ensuring that human systems are nested within and subordinate to ecological systems.

However, it provides a warning against the dangers of abstract, universalizing models that ignore local, territorial realities. Its limitation lies in its difficulty in scaling to complex, highly urbanized, and technologically advanced global societies without being co-opted or diluted, as has been observed in state-led implementations. It is highly useful for the Society, Civilization, and Planet scales.

## 10. Mapping to Provisional 7 Layers
| Layer | Rating | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Matter / Foundation | 2 | Deep connection to territory, land, water, and physical sustenance through harmonious agriculture and hunting. |
| Life / Vitality | 2 | Biocentrism is the core; all of nature is viewed as a living, interconnected organism. |
| Agency / Power | 1 | Agency is distributed across the community and nature, rather than concentrated in individual human will. |
| Connection / Cohesion | 2 | Reciprocity, solidarity, and the Ayllu (community) are the fundamental social glues. |
| Expression / Governance | 2 | Plurinationality, communal assemblies, and the legal recognition of the Rights of Nature. |
| Knowledge / Vision | 2 | Emphasizes ancestral wisdom, decolonization of knowledge, and epistemic pluralism. |
| Consciousness / Meaning | 2 | Deeply spiritual, viewing life as a pursuit of cosmic harmony and balance. |

*(Ratings: 2=strongly supported, 1=indirectly supported, 0=absent)*

## 11. Key Sources
[1] Acosta, A. (2011). *Buen Vivir: Sumak Kawsay, una oportunidad para imaginar otros mundos*.
[2] Gudynas, E. (2011). *Buen Vivir: Germinando alternativas al desarrollo*.
[3] Constitutions of Ecuador (2008) and Bolivia (2009).
[4] Bretón Solo de Zaldívar, V. (2017). *Three divergent understandings of Buen Vivir in the Ecuador of the Citizens’ Revolution*. Latin American and Caribbean Ethnic Studies.
[5] Villalba-Eguiluz, U., & Carranza-Barona, C. (2025). *Sustainability as Buen Vivir within a community-territory and the Rights of Nature*. Current Research in Environmental Sustainability.
[6] Waldmüller, J. M. (2014). *Buen Vivir, Sumak Kawsay, 'Good Living': An Introduction and Overview*. Alternautas.

## [G] Genuine Progress Indicator — GPI


## 1. Basic Identification
**Name:** Genuine Progress Indicator (GPI)

**Author / Origin:** The model was originally conceptualized as the Index of Sustainable Economic Welfare (ISEW) by Herman Daly and John Cobb in 1989. It was later refined and rebranded as the Genuine Progress Indicator by Clifford Cobb, Ted Halstead, and Jonathan Rowe at the think tank Redefining Progress in 1995.

**Period:** 1989 (as ISEW) / 1995 (as GPI) to the present.

**Field:** Ecological Economics, Green Economics, and Sustainability.

**Primary Scale:** Society / Nation / State.

## 2. Purpose
The Genuine Progress Indicator solves the fundamental problem of Gross Domestic Product (GDP) being misused as a proxy for societal well-being. GDP measures only the gross volume of monetary transactions, treating both positive activities (such as building a hospital) and negative activities (such as cleaning up an oil spill or treating diseases caused by pollution) as economic growth. 

The GPI was created for policymakers, economists, and citizens to provide a more accurate measure of true economic welfare and societal progress. It contextualizes economic growth by accounting for the social and environmental costs of that growth. By doing so, it distinguishes between uneconomic growth—where the costs to society and the environment exceed the benefits—and genuine progress.

## 3. Explicit Structure
The GPI is explicitly structured around a mathematical formula (GPI = A + B - C - D + I) that aggregates 26 specific indicators. These indicators are divided into three main categories: Economic, Environmental, and Social.

| Category | Indicators |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Economic** | Personal Consumption Expenditures (Base), Income Inequality (Gini index adjustment), Adjusted Personal Consumption, Cost of Consumer Durables, Value of Consumer Durables, Cost of Underemployment, Net Capital Investment. |
| **Environmental** | Cost of Water Pollution, Cost of Air Pollution, Cost of Noise Pollution, Loss of Wetlands, Loss of Farmland/soil quality, Loss of Primary Forest, CO2 Emissions/Cost of Climate Change, Cost of Ozone Depletion, Depletion of Non-Renewable Energy Resources. |
| **Social** | Value of Housework and Parenting (+), Cost of Family Changes/Divorce (-), Cost of Crime (-), Cost of Household Pollution Abatement (-), Value of Volunteer Work (+), Loss of Leisure Time (-), Value of Higher Education (+), Value of Highways and Streets (+), Cost of Commuting (-), Cost of Auto Accidents (-). |

## 4. Implicit Ontology
The GPI divides reality into a ledger of "benefits" (welfare-enhancing activities) and "costs" (welfare-reducing activities), categorizing them into economic, social, and environmental domains. Its fundamental building block is the monetary valuation of human and ecological activities. The model assumes that all relevant aspects of well-being, even non-market ones like volunteerism or ozone depletion, can and should be translated into a common financial metric to be added or subtracted from a baseline of consumption.

The model considers material consumption, ecological integrity (as a resource base and sink), social stability (family, crime), and time use (leisure, commuting, unpaid labor) as essential domains. Conversely, it considers subjective well-being, psychological states, and cultural expression as secondary, capturing them only indirectly through metrics like the cost of crime or loss of leisure. Furthermore, the GPI completely leaves out spiritual development, consciousness, deep meaning, artistic creation, civilizational purpose, and the non-anthropocentric intrinsic value of nature, viewing nature primarily as a "service" or "capital" for human welfare.

## 5. Layer Logic
The deep logic of the Genuine Progress Indicator is rooted in capital maintenance and sustainable income. It is based on Hicksian income theory, which posits that true income is the maximum amount that can be consumed without undermining the capacity to consume the same amount in the future. 

The logic flows from material consumption, which is adjusted for equity, augmented by non-market social goods, and finally penalized by social and environmental degradation. Ultimately, it is fundamentally an accounting logic of assets versus liabilities applied to the macro-societal level.

## 6. Covered Domains
| Domain | Rating | Domain | Rating | Domain | Rating |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| body/physical survival | 1 | economy | 2 | media | 0 |
| energy | 2 | work | 2 | communication | 0 |
| water | 2 | value creation | 2 | knowledge | 1 |
| food | 1 | entrepreneurship | 0 | science | 0 |
| health | 1 | finance | 1 | technology | 0 |
| habitat | 1 | governance | 0 | AI/computation | 0 |
| infrastructure | 2 | law/justice | 1 | ethics | 1 |
| mobility | 2 | security | 1 | meaning | 0 |
| materials | 2 | community | 2 | spirituality | 0 |
| ecology | 2 | relationships | 1 | consciousness | 0 |
| biodiversity | 1 | family | 2 | future/foresight | 2 |
| | | education | 2 | planetary direction | 1 |
| | | culture | 0 | civilizational purpose | 0 |
| | | art | 0 | | |

*(Ratings: 2=central, 1=present but secondary, 0=absent)*

## 7. Missing Domains
Important domains that are absent from the GPI include subjective well-being, psychological health, cultural vitality, artistic expression, governance quality, spiritual meaning, and civilizational purpose. This absence is a direct limitation of its methodology. Because the GPI relies on monetizing indicators to create a single composite index, it inherently excludes domains that are highly resistant to financial valuation. Consequently, the model remains trapped in the paradigm of *homo economicus*, albeit presenting a much broader and greener version of it.

## 8. Biases
The Genuine Progress Indicator exhibits several distinct biases. It has a strong economic and monetary bias, assuming that all value and loss can be quantified in financial terms. It is anthropocentric, valuing nature primarily for its utility to humans through ecosystem services and defensive costs, rather than recognizing its intrinsic worth. 

The model is also highly materialist, focusing on physical consumption, the physical environment, and time allocation, while completely ignoring interiority, consciousness, and spirituality. Finally, it is Western-centric and developmentalist, as it is rooted in Western economic accounting systems (modifying the UN System of National Accounts) and assumes a baseline of a consumption-driven society.

## 9. Contribution to Elysium
The major idea this model brings to the Elysium framework is the concept of "defensive expenditures" and "uneconomic growth"—the realization that much of what is traditionally counted as progress is actually the cost of fixing the damage caused by current systemic failures. 

The GPI could enrich the Elysium ontology through its explicit accounting of non-market value, such as unpaid care work and volunteerism, and its subtraction of systemic externalities like pollution, inequality, and crime from the definition of progress. However, it also provides a critical warning: attempting to fix GDP by simply adding more monetized variables still leaves society trapped in a transactional, accounting-based worldview that misses the qualitative depth of human and civilizational flourishing. The model is primarily useful for the Society scale as a macroeconomic policy tool.

## 10. Mapping to Provisional 7 Layers
| Layer | Rating | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Matter / Foundation | 2 | Strongly supported. Heavily focuses on resource depletion, physical infrastructure, and material consumption. |
| Life / Vitality | 2 | Strongly supported. Central focus on ecological integrity, pollution, wetlands, forests, and physical health impacts. |
| Agency / Power | 1 | Indirectly supported. Touches on economic agency via employment and capital, but lacks political or individual agency metrics. |
| Connection / Cohesion | 2 | Strongly supported. Explicitly measures income inequality, family breakdown, crime, and volunteerism. |
| Expression / Governance | 0 | Absent. Does not measure cultural expression, art, or the quality of governance. |
| Knowledge / Vision | 1 | Indirectly supported. Includes the value of higher education, but lacks broader metrics for science, foresight, or wisdom. |
| Consciousness / Meaning | 0 | Absent. Completely ignores interiority, spirituality, and deep meaning. |

*(Ratings: 2=strongly supported, 1=indirectly supported, 0=absent)*

## 11. Key Sources
Daly, H. E., & Cobb, J. B. (1989). *For the Common Good: Redirecting the Economy toward Community, the Environment, and a Sustainable Future*. Beacon Press.

Cobb, C., Halstead, T., & Rowe, J. (1995). *The Genuine Progress Indicator: Summary of Data and Methodology*. Redefining Progress.

Costanza, R., Erickson, J., Fligger, K., Adams, A., Adams, C., Altschuler, B., ... & Kelly, J. (2004). "Estimates of the Genuine Progress Indicator (GPI) for Vermont, Chittenden County and Burlington, from 1950 to 2000." *Ecological Economics*.

Lawn, P. A. (2003). "A theoretical foundation to support the Index of Sustainable Economic Welfare (ISEW), Genuine Progress Indicator (GPI), and other related indexes." *Ecological Economics*.

Talberth, J., Cobb, C., & Slattery, N. (2007). *The Genuine Progress Indicator 2006: A Tool for Sustainable Development*. Redefining Progress.


---

## [G] Capability Approach — Amartya Sen / Martha Nussbaum


## 1. Basic Identification
- **Name:** Capability Approach (10 Central Capabilities)
- **Author / Origin:** Amartya Sen and Martha Nussbaum
- **Period:** 1980s (Sen's initial formulation) to 2000s (Nussbaum's definitive list in *Women and Human Development*, 2000, and *Creating Capabilities*, 2011)
- **Field:** Development Economics, Political Philosophy, Ethics, Human Development
- **Primary Scale:** Individual / Society

## 2. Purpose
What problem does this model solve? For whom? In what context?
It solves the problem of measuring human well-being and development using purely economic metrics (like GDP per capita) or utilitarian metrics (happiness/preference satisfaction). It provides a framework for evaluating social justice, human rights, and policy effectiveness by focusing on what individuals are actually able to do and be (their substantive freedoms). It was developed for policymakers, development economists (e.g., the UNDP's Human Development Index), and political philosophers to ensure a minimum threshold of human dignity for all individuals, particularly marginalized groups and women.

## 3. Explicit Structure
What explicit categories, levels, stages, domains, or progression does the model use? List them all.
The model distinguishes between three core concepts:
- **Functionings:** The "beings and doings" of a person (e.g., being well-nourished, taking part in the community).
- **Capabilities:** The real opportunities or substantive freedoms to achieve these functionings.
- **Agency:** The ability to pursue goals one values.

Nussbaum's 10 Central Capabilities:
1. **Life:** Being able to live to the end of a human life of normal length.
2. **Bodily Health:** Being able to have good health, including reproductive health, adequate nourishment, and shelter.
3. **Bodily Integrity:** Being able to move freely, secure against assault, having opportunities for sexual satisfaction and reproductive choice.
4. **Senses, Imagination, and Thought:** Being able to use the senses, imagine, think, and reason, cultivated by adequate education, freedom of expression, and religious exercise.
5. **Emotions:** Being able to have attachments to things and people outside ourselves; to love, grieve, experience longing, gratitude, and justified anger.
6. **Practical Reason:** Being able to form a conception of the good and to engage in critical reflection about the planning of one's life.
7. **Affiliation:** (a) Being able to live with and toward others, recognize and show concern for other human beings, engage in social interaction. (b) Having the social bases of self-respect and non-humiliation (non-discrimination).
8. **Other Species:** Being able to live with concern for and in relation to animals, plants, and the world of nature.
9. **Play:** Being able to laugh, to play, to enjoy recreational activities.
10. **Control over one's Environment:** (a) Political: being able to participate effectively in political choices. (b) Material: being able to hold property, having the right to seek employment on an equal basis.

## 4. Implicit Ontology
- **How does this model divide reality?** It divides reality into the space of resources/commodities (means), the space of capabilities (freedoms/opportunities), and the space of functionings (actualized states of being/doing).
- **What are its fundamental "building blocks"?** The individual human being as an end in themselves (ethical individualism), human dignity, and substantive freedoms.
- **What domains does it consider essential?** Human agency, physical well-being, cognitive/emotional flourishing, social relationships, and political/material empowerment.
- **What does it consider secondary?** Economic growth, resources, income, and utility are considered strictly secondary—they are merely means to the end of expanding human capabilities.
- **What does it completely leave out?** It largely leaves out systemic/macro-structural evolution, cosmic/spiritual dimensions beyond individual meaning-making, and collective/civilizational teleology (it is highly individualistic in its normative focus).

## 5. Layer Logic
What is the deep logic? (material→subtle / need→fulfillment / individual→collective / simple→complex / interior→exterior / biological→cultural→spiritual / system→metasystem / other)
The deep logic is **potentiality → actualization** (Aristotelian roots) and **means → ends**. It moves from negative freedom (absence of interference) to positive freedom (substantive opportunity). The logic is non-hierarchical among the 10 capabilities; Nussbaum argues they are incommensurable and all 10 must be secured up to a minimum threshold for a life worthy of human dignity. It does not follow a strict developmental progression (like Maslow) but rather establishes a holistic baseline of human flourishing.

## 6. Covered Domains
Rate each (2=central, 1=present but secondary, 0=absent):
- body/physical survival: 2
- energy: 0
- water: 1 (implied in health/life)
- food: 2 (health)
- health: 2
- habitat: 2 (shelter/health)
- infrastructure: 1 (implied for mobility/health)
- mobility: 2 (bodily integrity)
- materials: 0
- ecology: 1 (other species)
- biodiversity: 1 (other species)
- economy: 1 (material control/employment)
- work: 2 (material control)
- value creation: 1
- entrepreneurship: 0
- finance: 0
- governance: 2 (political control)
- law/justice: 2 (affiliation/non-discrimination, political control)
- security: 2 (bodily integrity)
- community: 2 (affiliation)
- relationships: 2 (emotions, affiliation)
- family: 2 (emotions, affiliation)
- education: 2 (senses, imagination, thought)
- culture: 2 (senses, imagination, thought)
- art: 1 (play, imagination)
- media: 1 (freedom of expression)
- communication: 1
- knowledge: 2 (practical reason, thought)
- science: 0
- technology: 0
- AI/computation: 0
- ethics: 2 (practical reason)
- meaning: 2 (practical reason)
- spirituality: 1 (freedom of religion/thought)
- consciousness: 1 (senses, thought)
- future/foresight: 0
- planetary direction: 0
- civilizational purpose: 0

## 7. Missing Domains
What important domains are absent? Is this a limitation or simply out of scope?
Absent domains include technology, macro-economics, planetary-scale systems, civilizational evolution, and deep ecology (nature is viewed mostly through the lens of human concern/relationship). It lacks a systemic or institutional theory of *how* to provide these capabilities, focusing instead on the normative *what*. This is out of scope by design, as it is an evaluative framework for human development rather than a sociological or civilizational systems theory.

## 8. Biases
Identify biases: Western-centric? Spiritual? Materialist? Economic? Technocratic? Psychological? Ecological? Academic? Developmentalist? Anthropocentric? Elitist? New Age? Religious? Political?
- **Anthropocentric:** Highly focused on human dignity and human flourishing (though Nussbaum later extended it to animal rights).
- **Individualistic:** Evaluates justice strictly at the level of the individual, resisting collective metrics that might sacrifice individuals for the group.
- **Western-centric / Liberal:** Deeply rooted in Aristotelian ethics, Kantian dignity, and Western liberal political philosophy (though Sen draws on Indian philosophy and both argue for its universal cross-cultural applicability).
- **Developmentalist:** Framed primarily as a response to development economics and policy.
- **Academic:** Highly philosophical and conceptual.

## 9. Contribution to Elysium
- **What major idea does this model bring?** The distinction between means (resources/GDP) and ends (actual human freedoms/capabilities), and the concept of "incommensurable" core capabilities (you cannot compensate for a lack of political freedom with extra economic wealth).
- **What category could enrich the Elysium ontology?** The concept of "Agency" and "Substantive Freedom" as the ultimate metric of societal success, rather than mere systemic efficiency or resource accumulation.
- **What warning or limitation does it provide?** A warning against reducing human progress to single metrics (like GDP or utility) and against collective models that ignore the dignity and specific capabilities of the individual.
- **Useful for Human, Society, Civilization scale, or all three?** Highly useful for the Human and Society scales. Less directly applicable to the Civilization scale, except as a foundational ethical constraint.

## 10. Mapping to Provisional 7 Layers
Rate each (2=strongly supported, 1=indirectly supported, 0=absent) with justification:
- **Matter / Foundation:** 1 (Indirectly supported; resources are recognized as necessary means, but not the focus.)
- **Life / Vitality:** 2 (Strongly supported; Life, Bodily Health, and Bodily Integrity are the first three central capabilities.)
- **Agency / Power:** 2 (Strongly supported; Practical Reason and Control over one's Environment are central. Agency is the core of the entire approach.)
- **Connection / Cohesion:** 2 (Strongly supported; Affiliation, Emotions, and Other Species cover social and relational cohesion.)
- **Expression / Governance:** 2 (Strongly supported; Political control, freedom of expression, and non-discrimination are explicit requirements.)
- **Knowledge / Vision:** 2 (Strongly supported; Senses, Imagination, and Thought, along with Practical Reason, emphasize cognitive flourishing.)
- **Consciousness / Meaning:** 1 (Indirectly supported; Practical reason involves forming a conception of the good, but it doesn't delve into transpersonal or higher states of consciousness.)

## 11. Key Sources
List primary books, articles, official pages, academic papers, institutional reports.
- Sen, Amartya. *Development as Freedom* (1999).
- Sen, Amartya. *Inequality Reexamined* (1992).
- Nussbaum, Martha C. *Women and Human Development: The Capabilities Approach* (2000).
- Nussbaum, Martha C. *Creating Capabilities: The Human Development Approach* (2011).
- Nussbaum, Martha C. *Frontiers of Justice: Disability, Nationality, Species Membership* (2006).
- UNDP. *Human Development Reports* (1990-present, heavily influenced by Sen's approach).


---

## [G] Manfred Max-Neef — Human Scale Development


## 1. Basic Identification
**Name:** Manfred Max-Neef — Human Scale Development

**Author / Origin:** The model was developed by Manfred Max-Neef, Antonio Elizalde, and Martin Hopenhayn, under the auspices of the Development Alternatives Centre (CEPAUR) and the Dag Hammarskjöld Foundation.

**Period:** The framework was first published in 1986 in the journal *Development Dialogue* and later expanded into the 1991 book *Human Scale Development: Conception, Application and Further Reflections*.

**Field:** The model is situated at the intersection of Ecological Economics, Development Studies, Humanistic Economics, and the Ontology of Needs.

**Primary Scale:** Society / Community / Individual. The model explicitly focuses on the "Human Scale," bridging the micro-macro articulation, and emphasizing the community and society as the primary locus of sustainable development.

## 2. Purpose
The Human Scale Development model addresses the systemic failure of traditional, mechanistic, and GDP-driven economic development paradigms that erroneously equate human progress with economic growth and material accumulation. It seeks to solve the problem of "poverties" in the plural, moving beyond the singular metric of economic poverty. By recognizing that any fundamental human need that is not adequately satisfied reveals a specific human poverty, the model provides a diagnostic tool for societal well-being.

Originally developed for Latin American communities facing severe economic crises, marginalization, and the failures of both top-down state paternalism and neoliberalism, the model serves communities, policymakers, and development practitioners. It operates within the context of grassroots community development, acknowledging ecological limits and advocating for participatory democracy as the foundation for a human-centered future.

## 3. Explicit Structure
The model is explicitly structured around a comprehensive Matrix of Needs and Satisfiers, which intersects two distinct categories of human experience. The first category comprises Existential Needs, which represent the modes of human experience: Being (qualities and attributes), Having (institutions, norms, and tools), Doing (actions and verbs), and Interacting or *Estar* (settings, environments, and spaces). The second category consists of nine Axiological Needs, which are the fundamental human needs: Subsistence, Protection, Affection, Understanding, Participation, Idleness (Leisure/Recreation), Creation, Identity, and Freedom. While a tenth need, Transcendence, is sometimes discussed in later reflections, the core model strictly focuses on these nine.

Furthermore, the model explicitly categorizes the mechanisms through which these needs are met, known as Satisfiers, into five distinct types based on their systemic impact.

| Satisfier Type | Description | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Violators / Destroyers** | Claim to satisfy a need but ultimately destroy the possibility of satisfying it and other needs. | Arms race (claims to provide protection but destroys security and freedom). |
| **Pseudo-satisfiers** | Generate a false or incomplete sense of satisfaction without truly fulfilling the underlying need. | Status symbols (attempt to satisfy identity but fail to provide true meaning). |
| **Inhibiting satisfiers** | Over-satisfy one specific need while severely hindering the fulfillment of other needs. | Overprotective parenting (satisfies protection but inhibits freedom and understanding). |
| **Singular satisfiers** | Satisfy one specific need exactly, remaining neutral in their impact on other needs. | Insurance systems (satisfy protection without affecting other domains). |
| **Synergic satisfiers** | Satisfy a given need while simultaneously stimulating and contributing to the fulfillment of multiple other needs. | Participatory democracy (satisfies participation while enhancing freedom, identity, and understanding). |

## 4. Implicit Ontology
The implicit ontology of the model divides reality into the universal and the cultural. It posits that fundamental human needs are finite, few, and universal across all cultures and historical periods, representing the ontological core of human existence. In contrast, satisfiers are culturally and historically determined, representing the material and non-material ways different societies attempt to meet these universal needs. Human experience is thus divided into the dynamic interaction between the nine axiological needs and the four existential modes (Being, Having, Doing, Interacting).

The fundamental building blocks of this reality are human subjectivity, community relationships, ecological limits, participatory democracy, and self-reliance. The qualitative dimensions of life, such as affection, identity, and creation, are considered essential. Conversely, material accumulation, Gross Domestic Product, macroeconomic indicators, and top-down institutional structures are considered secondary; they are viewed merely as potential, and often flawed, satisfiers rather than ends in themselves.

The model completely leaves out the non-human world as an entity with its own intrinsic needs. While it is ecologically aware and respects the environment as the context (*Umwelt*) for human life, it remains highly anthropocentric. It also lacks a detailed structural analysis of global geopolitical power dynamics, focusing instead on local and community self-reliance.

## 5. Layer Logic
The deep logic of the model is systemic and non-hierarchical. In direct contrast to Maslow's hierarchy of needs, Max-Neef posits that fundamental human needs are interrelated, interactive, and simultaneous. With the exception of subsistence, which is a biological prerequisite for life, there is no hierarchical progression. The logic operates on a continuum from need to fulfillment via satisfiers, viewed as a complex, synergistic web rather than a linear path.

Additionally, the model operates on an interior-to-exterior logic, moving from the universal human essence (needs) to culturally constructed external manifestations (satisfiers). It also relies on an individual-collective dynamic, asserting that needs must be satisfied in relation to oneself (*Eigenwelt*), the social group (*Mitwelt*), and the environment (*Umwelt*).

## 6. Covered Domains
The model's coverage of various domains reflects its focus on human-scale development and community well-being.

| Domain | Rating | Justification |
| --- | --- | --- |
| body/physical survival | 2 | Central to the fundamental need for Subsistence. |
| energy | 1 | Implicitly present within Subsistence and environmental contexts. |
| water | 2 | Central to the fundamental need for Subsistence. |
| food | 2 | Central to the fundamental need for Subsistence. |
| health | 2 | Central to the fundamental needs for Subsistence and Protection. |
| habitat | 2 | Central to Protection and the existential category of Interacting/Spaces. |
| infrastructure | 1 | Present as a secondary element, categorized under Having (satisfiers). |
| mobility | 1 | Implicitly present within the existential category of Interacting. |
| materials | 1 | Present only as potential satisfiers, not as fundamental needs. |
| ecology | 2 | Central as the *Umwelt* (environment) context required for human survival. |
| biodiversity | 1 | Implicitly present within the model's broader ecological awareness. |
| economy | 2 | Central to the model, which seeks to redefine economics to a Human Scale. |
| work | 2 | Central to the fundamental needs for Creation, Subsistence, and Doing. |
| value creation | 2 | Central to Creation and the concept of Synergic satisfiers. |
| entrepreneurship | 1 | Present as a component of self-reliance and Creation. |
| finance | 1 | Present primarily as a critiqued macro-context rather than a core focus. |
| governance | 2 | Central to the fundamental needs for Participation and Freedom. |
| law/justice | 1 | Present as a secondary element under Protection and Having. |
| security | 2 | Central to the fundamental need for Protection. |
| community | 2 | Central to Participation, Affection, and Identity. |
| relationships | 2 | Central to Affection and the existential category of Interacting. |
| family | 2 | Central to Affection and Protection. |
| education | 2 | Central to the fundamental need for Understanding. |
| culture | 2 | Central to Identity and the cultural determination of satisfiers. |
| art | 2 | Central to the fundamental needs for Creation and Idleness. |
| media | 1 | Present as a secondary element, often critiqued as a pseudo-satisfier. |
| communication | 2 | Central to Understanding and Participation. |
| knowledge | 2 | Central to the fundamental need for Understanding. |
| science | 1 | Present as a secondary element under Understanding. |
| technology | 1 | Present as a satisfier, often critiqued when exogenous or non-human scale. |
| AI/computation | 0 | Absent from the model's historical and conceptual scope. |
| ethics | 2 | Central to the distinction between Synergic and Violator satisfiers. |
| meaning | 2 | Central to the fundamental needs for Identity and Understanding. |
| spirituality | 1 | Present marginally; Transcendence was debated but excluded from the core 9. |
| consciousness | 1 | Present as a secondary element under Understanding and Being. |
| future/foresight | 1 | Present in the model's focus on sustainable, alternative futures. |
| planetary direction | 1 | Implicitly present within the model's ecological limits. |
| civilizational purpose | 1 | Present in the model's ambition to redefine the purpose of development. |

## 7. Missing Domains
The model exhibits several notable absences, primarily due to its specific focus and historical context. The most significant limitation is the absence of Non-Human and Planetary Needs. The model is explicitly a *Human* Scale Development framework; while it respects the environment, it does not articulate the fundamental needs of the biosphere, ecosystems, or other species independent of human survival.

Furthermore, the model lacks a robust framework for Transcendence and Spirituality. Although Max-Neef debated including "Transcendence" as a tenth need, it was ultimately left out of the core matrix, rendering the model somewhat secular and humanist. The model also fails to account for Technological Evolution, treating technology strictly as a tool (satisfier) rather than an autonomous evolutionary force. Finally, it lacks a detailed ontology of Macro-Structural Power, focusing heavily on micro and meso-level community resilience while largely ignoring global geopolitical and military power dynamics.

## 8. Biases
The model is deeply Anthropocentric, centering its entire ontology exclusively on human needs. It carries a strong Anti-Capitalist and Anti-Neoliberal bias, fundamentally rejecting GDP-driven, consumerist, and top-down economic models. Consequently, it is highly Localist and Communitarian, favoring decentralization, local self-reliance, and grassroots participatory democracy over state or global governance structures.

Philosophically, the model is Humanist and Psychological, rooting development in human subjectivity and existential well-being rather than material infrastructure. Finally, it is profoundly shaped by its Latin American and Post-Colonial origins, deeply informed by the regional experience of dependency, dictatorship, and the systemic failure of exogenous modernization projects.

## 9. Contribution to Elysium
The Human Scale Development model brings a major conceptual breakthrough to the Elysium ontology: the absolute decoupling of Universal Needs (which are finite and ontological) from Cultural Satisfiers (which are infinite, historical, and material or immaterial). It introduces the critical concepts of "Synergic Satisfiers" and "Violators," providing a nuanced vocabulary for understanding how actions and structures impact human well-being. Furthermore, it redefines poverty as "poverties" in the plural, based on unmet non-material needs.

The model's typology of Satisfiers serves as a powerful diagnostic tool that could significantly enrich the Elysium ontology, allowing for the evaluation of civilizational structures based on their systemic impact. The matrix intersection of Existential and Axiological needs provides a robust grid for mapping human agency. However, the model provides a clear warning: civilization must not confuse material accumulation with need satisfaction. Its primary limitation is its anthropocentrism, requiring Elysium to supplement it with a planetary and ecological ontology that grants agency to the biosphere. The model is highly useful for the Human and Society scales, and serves as a vital diagnostic tool at the Civilization scale.

## 10. Mapping to Provisional 7 Layers
The model maps onto the provisional 7 layers of the Elysium ontology with varying degrees of support.

| Layer | Rating | Justification |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Matter / Foundation** | 1 | Recognizes Subsistence and the physical environment, but explicitly attempts to move beyond material foundations as the sole metric of development. |
| **Life / Vitality** | 2 | Strongly supported; Subsistence, physical health, and mental health are core components of the axiological matrix. |
| **Agency / Power** | 2 | Strongly supported; Freedom, Participation, and Creation are fundamental needs, and the model heavily emphasizes self-reliance and bottom-up agency. |
| **Connection / Cohesion** | 2 | Strongly supported; Affection, Identity, and the existential category of Interacting are central to the model's communitarian focus. |
| **Expression / Governance** | 2 | Strongly supported; Participation, Creation, and the critique of exogenous satisfiers directly address how humans express themselves and govern their communities. |
| **Knowledge / Vision** | 2 | Strongly supported; Understanding is a fundamental need, and the model highly values critical conscience, curiosity, and rationality. |
| **Consciousness / Meaning** | 1 | Indirectly supported; Identity and Understanding touch on meaning, but the explicit lack of a Transcendence layer limits this domain. |

## 11. Key Sources
Max-Neef, M., Elizalde, A., & Hopenhayn, M. (1986). *Human Scale Development: An Option for the Future*. Development Dialogue, 1989(1), 7-80. Dag Hammarskjöld Foundation.

Max-Neef, M. A. (1991). *Human Scale Development: Conception, Application and Further Reflections*. New York: The Apex Press.

Gasper, D. (2023). *Understanding Max-Neef's model of human needs as a practical toolkit for supporting development work and societal transitions*. In Beyond Ecological Economics and Development. Taylor & Francis.

Cruz, I., Stahel, A., & Max-Neef, M. (2009). *Towards a systemic development approach: Building on the Human-Scale Development paradigm*. Ecological Economics, 68(7), 2021-2030.
